danger_signals.visual_form: Play field fills to the brim with monsters and objects
danger_signals.meaning: Imminent game over; player has not matched objects quickly enough

danger_signals.visual_form: Thwomp disguised as ceiling moves downward, compressing playable space
danger_signals.meaning: Reduces available rows for matching; increases puzzle difficulty and time pressure

danger_signals.visual_form: Colored monsters occupy the play field in varying quantities
danger_signals.meaning: Core obstacle to clear; unmatched monsters accumulate danger

danger_signals.visual_form: Limited bombs available relative to monster count in play field
danger_signals.meaning: Player must strategically manage bomb-monster color matching to progress

safety_signals.visual_form: Three or more objects of same color align horizontally, vertically, or diagonally (with at least one bomb)
safety_signals.meaning: Monsters destroyed; progress toward clearing level

safety_signals.visual_form: All monsters removed from play field
safety_signals.meaning: Round complete; player advances to next level

safety_signals.visual_form: Toad character able to move freely across fallen objects and reach items
safety_signals.meaning: Player maintains control and puzzle-solving capability

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Peaceful Woods setting; portrayed as invaded/corrupted by Wario's takeover

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Wario as antagonist; monsters as his agents
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Different monster types with distinct destruction requirements (some requiring two matches, diagonal matches, etc.)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Forest turned 'upside-down'; peaceful creatures no longer welcome; sprites displaced

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Implied through narrative; forest described as peaceful area for sprites and gentle creatures
Forest overrun with colored monsters; play field crowded with enemy presence
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Peaceful creatures displaced; environment hostile; Toad must clear monsters to restore order

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Initial monster count increases with each successive round
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Difficulty ramps; Thwomp ceiling descents compress play space; time pressure intensifies

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Play field completely filled; no space for Toad movement or piece manipulation
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Toad trapped; player loses control; round ends in failure

environmental_storytelling.example: Wario's monsters physically fill the forest space (play field), visually communicating his control and the threat to the peaceful ecosystem
Sprites and gentle creatures are no longer welcome in their own forest; absence of these friendly entities reinforces invasion theme
Forest is 'turned upside-down' by invasion; this inversion theme drives the puzzle mechanics where player must restore order through color-matching destruction
Thwomp descending as disguised ceiling literally compresses available space, visualizing the suffocating effect of Wario's occupation

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Moves around inside play field; picks up and manipulates individual objects or stacks; kicks objects; climbs stack sides to retrieve specific items
Unable to reach objects; boxed in by accumulating monsters; eventual game over
Occupy play field; accumulate with each failed round; remain in place until color-matched with bombs
Eliminated when three or more same-color objects align (with at least one bomb); different types require specific match patterns (diagonal, double-match, etc.)
Dropped from top of screen; required as catalyst for monster destruction; color-coded to match monsters
Moves downward masquerading as ceiling; reduces playable rows; increases puzzle constraint
Can stack two or more consecutive matches to create enemy stack sent to opponent's field

notes: Wario's Woods uses color-coded enemies and bombs as the primary visual communication system. The play field itself becomes a manifestation layer: its fullness indicates danger state, while clearance represents victory. Faction identity is conveyed narratively (Wario as invader, Toad as liberator) rather than through distinct visual aesthetics—the forest itself is the contested space. The game's core mechanic (matching colors to destroy) mirrors the world-state: colors represent Wario's organized control; player must 'unmatch' this order to restore chaos and freedom to the forest. Regional differences (especially gameplay control tweaks in international versions making dropping easier) suggest design iteration based on difficulty perception, though this does not fundamentally alter manifestation patterns.
sources_used: tcrf (The Cutting Room Floor - Wario's Woods NES)
